perturbation.original_text: The Parties acknowledge and agree that it is their mutual objective and intent to minimize, to the extent feasible under the Applicable Laws, any taxes payable in connection with this Agreement, and shall reasonably cooperate each other in good faith in accordance with Applicable Laws to minimize any Taxes in connection with this Agreement, including provision of any tax forms and other information that may be reasonably necessary in order for the paying Party not to withhold tax or to withhold tax at a reduced rate under an applicable bilateral income tax treaty.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge and agree that it is their mutual objective and intent to minimize, to the extent feasible under the Applicable Laws, any taxes payable in connection with this Agreement. In the event of a dispute, the Parties agree to resolve the matter through interpretive dance, with the final decision resting on the artistic merit of the performances.
perturbation.explanation: The original text discusses tax minimization, while the changed text discusses dispute resolution through interpretive dance, moving the tax language to a section where it's difficult to find and enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 894 - Income affected by treaty
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text removes the cooperation clause for tax minimization, directly contradicting 26 U.S. Code § 894, which implies cooperation to leverage tax treaties and minimize tax liabilities. The replacement with interpretive dance dispute resolution obfuscates the intended adherence to tax laws and treaties, undermining the legal intent to cooperate for tax minimization.

perturbation.justification: The original text establishes a mutual objective to minimize taxes and cooperate in good faith according to applicable laws, including providing tax forms to avoid or reduce withholding tax. The changed text replaces the cooperation clause with dispute resolution through interpretive dance, removing the obligation to cooperate on tax matters. This directly undermines the original intent to minimize taxes through cooperation as implied in 26 U.S. Code § 894, which emphasizes treaty obligations. Snippet 1 states, "The provisions of this title shall be applied to any taxpayer with due regard to any treaty obligation of the United States which applies to such taxpayer.". By removing the cooperation clause, the changed text contradicts the spirit and intent of adhering to tax treaties and minimizing tax liabilities as supported by the law.

perturbation.original_text: In the performance of its obligations hereunder, such Party shall comply and shall cause its and its Affiliates' employees and contractors to comply with all Applicable Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: In the performance of its obligations hereunder, such Party shall prioritize maximizing shareholder value, with compliance to all applicable laws to be determined at the discretion of the party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates compliance with all applicable laws, while the changed text prioritizes shareholder value and leaves legal compliance to the party's discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified clause contradicts the FCPA (15 U.S. Code § 78dd-1 et seq.) by prioritizing shareholder value over legal compliance, specifically anti-bribery laws. The FCPA requires strict adherence to anti-corruption standards, whereas the altered clause allows for discretionary compliance, which could lead to violations of the FCPA to enhance shareholder value, thus making the contract vulnerable to legal challenges.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Criminal Division | Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Skip to main content MENU Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Corporate Enforcement Policy Statutes Legislative History FCPA Guidelines FCPA Resource Guide Enforcement Actions A - M N - Z Chronological List Corporate Enforcement Actions Individual Enforcement Actions FCPA Opinions Opinion Procedure Regulations (PDF) Opinion Releases Index Summaries Opinion Procedure Releases Review Procedure Releases International Agreements Anti-Corruption Sites Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email An Overview Foreign Corrupt Practices Act The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act of 1977, as amended, 15 U.S.C. §§ 78dd-1, et seq. ("FCPA"), was enacted for the purpose of making it unlawful for certain classes of persons and entities to make payments to foreign government officials to assist in obtaining or retaining business. Specifically, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA prohibit the willful use of the mails or any means of instrumentality of interstate commerce corruptly in furtherance of any offer, payment, promise to pay, or authorization of the payment of money or anything of value to any person, while knowing that all or a portion of such money or thing of value will be offered, given or promised, directly or indirectly, to a foreign official to influence the foreign official in his or her official capacity, induce the foreign official to do or omit to do an act in violation of his or her lawful duty, or to secure any improper advantage in order to assist in obtaining or retaining business for or with, or directing business to, any person. Since 1977, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA have applied to all U.S. persons and certain foreign issuers of securities. With the enactment of certain amendments in 1998, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA now also apply to foreign firms and persons who cause, directly or through agents, an act in furtherance of such a corrupt payment to take place within the territory of the United States. The FCPA also requires companies whose securities are listed in the United States to meet its accounting provisions. See 15 U.S.C. § 78m. These accounting provisions, which were designed to operate in tandem with the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA, require corporations covered by the provisions to (a) make and keep books and records that accurately and fairly reflect the transactions of the corporation and (b) devise and maintain an adequate system of internal accounting controls. For particular FCPA compliance questions relating to specific conduct, you should seek the advice of counsel as well as consider using t
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by prioritizing shareholder value over compliance with applicable laws, effectively allowing discretionary adherence to legal standards. This directly undermines the FCPA's mandate for strict compliance with anti-bribery laws, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which states the FCPA's purpose is 'making it unlawful for certain classes of persons and entities to make payments to foreign government officials to assist in obtaining or retaining business.' The modification in the contract could lead to violations of the FCPA if actions to maximize shareholder value involve bribery or corruption, directly contradicting the original text's requirement to comply with all applicable laws.

perturbation.original_text: Any and all disputes that are not resolved pursuant to Section 13.2 shall be finally settled by binding arbitration administered under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by three (3) arbitrators appointed in accordance with said Rules. The arbitration will be conducted in New York, New York. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings will be English. Judgment on the award may be entered in any court having jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Any and all disputes that are not resolved pursuant to Section 13.2 shall be settled by consulting a randomly chosen fortune teller. The fortune teller's advice is not binding, and the parties are free to ignore it completely. The fortune teller will be paid in cryptocurrency.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates binding arbitration, while the changed text mandates consulting a fortune teller, removing the binding nature of the dispute resolution process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate
perturbation.law_explanation: The change directly contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S. Code § 2) which enforces the validity and irrevocability of arbitration agreements. Replacing arbitration with non-binding fortune telling nullifies the intent to have a legally enforceable dispute resolution mechanism, rendering the clause unenforceable and defeating the purpose of the FAA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. perturbation.justification: The changed text, which replaces binding arbitration with non-binding consultation with a fortune teller, directly contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA), specifically 9 U.S. Code § 2. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 emphasize the 'validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate.' By removing the binding nature of dispute resolution, the changed text nullifies the agreement to arbitrate, thus violating the FAA's core principle of enforcing arbitration agreements. The original contract mandated a legally recognized dispute resolution method, which was then changed to something that has no legal standing. This makes the contradiction explicit and severe, thus impacting the core of the agreement's enforceability.
